title: End of the Iran War: a New Balance of Power in the Middle East | Hamidreza Azizi

segments.text: What's up everybody? My name is Dmitri Kaffinis and you're listening to Hidden Forces,
a podcast that inspires investors, entrepreneurs and everyday citizens to challenge consensus narratives,
and learn how to think critically, but the systems of power shaping our world.
My guest in this episode of Hidden Forces is Hamid Reza Azizi, an expert on Iranian foreign policy
and the author of the Axis of Resistance, Iran, Israel, and the struggle for the Middle East.
We spent the first hour of our conversation today picking up where Hamid Reza and I
left off when he was last on the podcast, only a few weeks into the commencement of Operation
Epic Fury. We discussed the transformation of Iran's governance structure following the
assassination of its supreme leader Ali Hamine, the contours of the memorandum of understanding,
signed between the United States and Iran, and how it compares to the JCPOA, signed by Obama in
2015. The question of whether Iran still needs a nuclear weapon at all or whether demonstrated
control over the straightforward moves has become its functional equivalent. The role of Hezbollah
and Lebanon as the most volatile variable in any lasting peace arrangement, and an honest accounting
of what Iran has lost in this war. And why those losses, not just its leverage,
are what have driven Tehran to the negotiating table. The second hour turns to the broader regional
and geopolitical consequences of what has transpired over the last few months.
We deepen our discussion about the changing nature of the Iranian regime and what that means
for its relationship with the United States and other countries in the region before moving to a
series of conversations about Israel, the Gulf States, Saudi Arabia, and China. We discuss
Netanyahu's political future, the future of the US is really alliance, the implications for Saudi
Arabia and other Gulf states, along with their financial inflows, and their attractiveness as
destinations for wealthy expats and human capital, and conclude with a discussion about China,
its ambitions across its western horizon, and whether a peace agreement between the US and Iran
is truly a strategic defeat for the United States, or if in fact it may herald a new era of
political and geo-strategic realignment in the Middle East that could prove to be more stabilizing
and less violent than what we have learned to live with in recent decades. If you want access
to all of this conversation, go to hiddenforces.io, slash subscribe, and join our premium feed,
which you can listen to on your mobile device using your favorite podcast app just like you're
listening to this episode right now. If you want to join in on the conversation become a member of
the Hidden Forces Genius Community, which includes Q&A calls with guests, discounted access
to their party research and analysis, and in person events like our intimate dinners and weekend
retreats, you can also do that on our subscriber page. If you still have questions, feel free to
send an email to infoathiddenforces.io and I or someone from our team will get right back to you.
And with that, please enjoy this incredibly comprehensive and timely conversation with my guest,
Hamidreza Azizi. Hamidreza Azizi, welcome back to Hidden Forces.
Thank you for relicing me again. It's the pleasure of joining you.
I'm excited to have you back on the program. So I'd like to begin this conversation by
broadening the context to include our last discussion when you were first in the podcast, which
was almost three weeks into the commencement of Operation Epic Fury, and which came three weeks
before Trump. Some may remember announced that he had achieved total and complete victory
following the ceasefire deal with Iran that immediately fell apart in which led to seemingly
unending series of dubious and contradictory statements from the White House about
the imminence of a new deal and all sorts of other confusing statements.
At that time, Iran was still targeting Gulf infrastructure, including US military bases,
signaling its commitment to fighting what appeared at that time, at least to be an open-ended
war of attrition. Looking back on that conversation in the context of everything that has transpired
over the last three months, how did your expectations then match up with how negotiations have
progressed since and the way in which this war of attrition hasn't folded? Well, I think the
counters of what we have seen over the past few weeks after the cessation of
active warfare and during the ceasefire was already evidence when we were discussing, because
I remember that we discussed the reasons actually for the start of the war and the expectations
on the side of the US and Israel of a very quick and decisive victory, but three weeks into the war,
it was I think already clear that things were going differently and not as expected by President
Trump and also Benjamin Netanyahu. So, I think Iran managed to somehow eventually turn it
into a war of attrition in which not only through targeting the US military's infrastructure
in the region and also through attacks on Israel itself, especially in the second half of the war,
but also through the effective closure of the state of war most, to impose costs on the US that
eventually led to the ceasefire and now to the memorandum of understanding that was initially
signed, but it's ironically understood to be signed on Friday. So this mismatch, I think, between
the initial expectations of initiators of the war and the realities on the ground and the actual
capabilities of Iran, I think, was already clear. So, this is how I remember our conversation,
and I think that's only proven to be the case since then. So, just again to remind people of the
context, this was when the United States was actively engaged in taking out just layers upon layers
of Iranian leadership. Of course, they took out the supreme leader to initiate the operation
epic fury on February 28th. At that time, you raised doubts about whether there was anyone left
in the Islamic Republic with the political authority and standing to negotiate a durable settlement
that would be accepted by all the relevant factions holding power within the country. Has anything
changed within Iran's leadership structure since then that makes the prospects of peace
more likely today in your view? I think the oldest structure changed. So, back then, we were discussing
about the individuals within the system. What's eventually transpired was somehow the
transformation of the system itself. And I have discussed this at length in an article that
wrote last months for the time magazine. And to put it in an article, actually, a system which was
very much vertical and hierarchical on the Ali Khomeini, the former supreme leader,
in which the Islamic Republic, indeed, had some institutions like quasi-democratic institutions
and like the parliament, like the institution, like the Supreme National Security Council. But
everything, all the decisions which were on strategic matters like war, peace, nuclear diplomacy,
etc would have to go through the Supreme Leader and his endorsement. So he had the lethal power
and also, importantly, it was the final arbiter in the disputes that you know could arise between
different parts of the government and the political system. So, after him and also after
some heavyweights like Adelaide Johnny, for example, were also removed during the U.S. and Israeli
strikes, the system started to quality somehow around its security core. And what we are seeing
right now is leadership that is very much military security oriented. I hesitate to call it
military system, for example, because it's different and a military counter, for example, you know,
as you know, kind of in the standard sense of the words, there are people who are close to their
revolutionary guards, former commanders, including by the way, the current chief negotiator and
the parliament, the Speaker Mama Bagan-Khalibov, was also the current commander-in-chief of the IRGC.
And some people like the Chief Justice, but almost any of the AGE, who's also from the
within the security establishment. And the new supremely, the worst of a holiday, is one
along the few, I think. She doesn't have the same authority, neither in terms of political
influence, nor religious acceptance within the critical circles that his father has. So, in that
sense, in a way, making a strategic decision within the system takes time. And that was the reason,
for example, that it took a lot of back-and-forths and a lot of mediated diplomacy until we reached
what we have right now that we're on to our understanding. But one can argue that because it has
the support of the core elements of the system, especially the powerful IRGC. I mean, we can expect
that it's going to hold at least as far as Iran is concerned. And we have already seen signs of
that. If we compare the current memorandum and I must say that it is very initial, it is only the
starting point, not the end point. But if we compare it with the 2015 nuclear deal, the JCPOA,
the IRGC actually was the party at the time that was somehow trying to disrupt this. It was
partly out of the faction of rivalry with the so-called moderates camp that by former president
Hassan Rouhani, on the other hand, out of this kind of rigid ideological, I'm at the same time
strategic considerations of confrontation with the United States. But this time, so far, in the
past couple of days since the announcement of the memorandum understanding, the deputy commander
of IRGC on political affairs, for example. And then Klaatemoulambia center of headquarters,
which is in charge of coordinating and carrying on armed forces, then the head of the
Wotesforces, Lailani, and the commander of the IRGC Air Force. All of them have come out either
directly or tacitably, basically backing the diplomatic team. So these are the developments
that we have seen. So people within the system seem to be kind of inagreement. Right now that
this is the West past forward, not necessarily that it's going to be eventually quite successful,
but consensus within the system means that at least there's curiosity how this should proceed.
So actually, my next question was going to be what has created the conditions for the signing of
this recently great upon MOU. But more specifically, I want to clarify, because it sounds like the
leadership changes as a result of this war of attrition. And in some cases, targeted assassinations,
in many cases, of elements of both the Islamic Republic's leadership as well as the IRGC's
leadership, it seems that that has also played a role in creating the conditions for this
latest MOU on the Iranian side. Is that fair to clarify?
Absolutely. Look, this transformation within the Islamic Republic, the way from
political dominance and towards more RGC dominance or military security dominance was already
under way. I would say at least since 2009, seems the green movement protest, which were to give
some context, the protest, which was triggered by the rigged elections that kind of, you know,
allowed Mahodam Adina Shah, the former president, to stay in power for the second time. People
went to the streets in support of the opposition figure, Mir Jose Moussavi at the time.
Anyway, at that time already, people started speaking the source of an IRGC coup.
I mean, coup d'état, maybe is not the best force to describe it, but in the sense of, you know,
gradually, or as my colleague Ali Al-Sona puts it, a creeping coup by the IRGC that's, you know,
took shape over almost two decades, more than two decades. It was more or less what actually happened.
So the security establishments around the IRGC started somehow to increase, not only their
political power, but also increasingly economic power, they started to dominate foreign policy as well.
And the election, or I would say, selection of Iran Reisi was a turning point here in 2021,
which tilted the balance completely toward the IRGC. Reisi himself was a cleric,
but he was rather a weak figure, again, endorsed by Ali Hamadi on one hand, and the security
establishments on the other. Anyway, password was to this war. Ali Hamadi himself was the figure
who was playing the balancing role between these two pillars. And the one had, he himself, had
sort of cultivated the IRGC in his current form as a main pillar of his role. And at the end
of the day, we shouldn't forget that even as even the name suggests, it is the guardians of the
horizontal revolution. So created for this very purpose of supporting the regime and kind of
helping the regime survive. And on the other hand, of course, him being a cleric, basically keeping
that part as well. So here's an immobile, paved the way somehow for a quick takeover by that very
security establishment, which was quite entrenched within the system. Most of our harmony is
himself a cleric, but he's better known or he's networked within the IRGC and the kind of
security system. So it was somehow an opportunity that they took in order to finalize and formalize
what was already going on. So in an ironic way, I would say, the war contributed to this
transformation and this kind of the state of affairs that we are seeing at the moment.
Can any of what has transpired here be described as regime change?
I have to say to call it a regime change, because at the end of the day, all the people who are
now within the kind of, you know, leadership, let's say, or the people who are core decision
measures, they are the same people who've been within the system, you know, holding very senior
positions for decades. Like, take what you offer, for example, he's been almost everywhere
within the system from the commander of the IRGC's aerospace force to the commander of the police,
to the mayor of the around capital to the Speaker of Parliament. You ran four times if I'm not
for president, failed each time. But now he is basically one of the key decision makers. So he's
the case, so I'm not like you need the current IRGC commander in chief. He was actually the first
commander of the world's force, the one that is known more about Soleimani, but he was established
by this guy. So when I'm saying that these people have somehow been expanding their power within
the system and have shifted this balance away from political system towards the military,
it's indicate a shift in the nature of the system or the regime and not a regime change,
because the regime change would be totally different. I mean, it is still authoritarian,
even more than before, still closed in terms of the circle of the elites in power. The only
thing as I said is transition from a more vertical decision-making system at the top to a more horizontal
one. That's the way I describe it. So we'll have a chance to dig into much more detail with respect
to the changes that have taken place within Iran. But let's just fast forward a second to the
actual MOU and clarify what we know in terms of the details associated with the document that's
going to be signed in person in Geneva on Friday. They already signed a digital copy,
but both sides will be meeting in a couple of days. And again, I don't know if I mentioned this,
but we're recording this on Wednesday, June 17th. What do we know about what has been discussed,
the contours of any potential deal, and what has been primarily agreed to?
I saw that CNN has published the text of the MOU every year today, and it is actually the same
text that I saw on Friday, even before the announcement of the agreements in some Iranian sources,
like on social media, and then also some of the media published the contours of it. So
both texts are kind of word-by-word similar. So my impression is that it is going to be the final one.
So there is, it's kind of a 14 articles in this text. It is a short one as Vice President
J.D. Wants said, one page or one page and a half, something like that. We just start
by expressing commitments by both sides of a permanent end to the war and refusal to
resort to the threat of war or use of force against each other in the future,
respecting each other's sovereignty, territorial integrity, etc. Then there are some provisions
about the lifting of U.S. naval blockades, which has been their ceasefire actually and during
the ceasefire, and on the Iran side, the Iranians committing to reopen the threat of almost
to commercial shipping. We didn't have a window of 30 days. There are some provisions about
negotiations, future negotiations, rewarding the Iran nuclear program, also the lifting of sanctions,
and so on. There is a 60-day window which is mentioned in the text as a period which starts by
the official signing of the documents in which two sides are going to negotiate the details,
including sanctions, removal, including the details of the Iranian nuclear concessions.
And then eventually article 14 says that the final deal that's going to come out of this
is going to be ratified at the U.S. Security Council as a binding resolution. But what I can see
in this text is that the only actual commitments by both sides that can be understood from this
is the sort of removal of this double blockade that's been there. So that is the only thing that is
clearly sort of mentioned in this. Other than that, almost everything is just some general
understandings, and that's why I think it's called the war on the understanding. And the details of
each of them is going to be determined in the diffusion negotiations. So it is broad enough
for both sides I think to be able to claim victory, but it is at the same time ambiguous enough
and potentially leave room for misunderstandings, miscalculations, and eventually
failures to reach any actual final comprehensive agreement. So this is my impression from the
text that is available right now. Yeah, that's another line of inquiry that I wanted to follow,
which is essentially how seriously should we take these talks and the commitment to work out
a potential deal along the preliminary terms outlined in this MOU. But before we discuss any of that,
let's go back to the nuclear question because when we last spoke, you raised the possibility that
the war had actually increased Iran's incentives to acquire a nuclear weapon, and you weren't alone
in that both because it demonstrated the very real costs associated with not having a nuclear
deterrent, and also because there's strategy of staying just below the nuclear threshold of breakout
was treated by the US and Israel as being indistinguishable from trying to urgently develop a nuclear
weapon, miniaturize it, and turn it to a warhead that could be used to strike the US or attack any of
its interests in the region. So my question for you is does the leverage that Iran has to close the
strait, demonstrate to its leadership that it no longer really needs to obtain a nuclear weapon
because a threatened closure of the strait is a more economical, less complicated and safer
equivalent of nuclear deterrence with most of the benefits associated in none of the costs.
There's debates ongoing in Iran between the experts and observers close to the
governments which I think is kind of a reflection also of different trends in terms of thinking within
the leadership, and it has to do with both elements of your question first about whether this war
has actually necessitated or sort of approved importance of acquiring a nuclear weapon for Iran
and second whether the strait of war most actually can be replacement for nuclear deterrence.
So on the first question since we spoke, I see kind of growing voices within Iran's strategic
community, not at the official level, that's an important distinction. But within Iran's strategic
community, those people who's thinking is believed to be impactful on the leadership's
sort of approach, and I said there's growing voices advocating for the developments of nuclear
weapon. And despite some counter-arguments, for example, that look, Israel has nuclear weapons,
but we managed to target Israel rather successfully, and you know, it didn't deter us,
I mean the Iranians, from the time Israel and so on and so forth, but they say at least
having nuclear weapons would guarantee that there's not going to be an existential threat
to the system and to the country. And they refer to two things. First, obviously, the killing of
the Supreme Leader, I see this kind of quite often, you know, comparing this, for example, I mean,
comparing Iran to North Korea, to Russia. Can you imagine, for example, even in a scenario of a
fierce war between Russia and the United States or North Korea and the United States that the US
would target the leadership of Russia, for example, Putin or Kim Jong Un, why? Because they have
nuclear weapons. So these are the arguments that are already there, but the thing is when it comes
to the actual capabilities, and when it comes to the technical assessment of what Iran has.
And again, here we only can refer to the open source data and the sort of reports provided by
IAEA, so long as they had access to the sites on the ground, and also intelligence assessment
for US and Egypt. Iran doesn't have the infrastructure for weaponization at the moment, at least quick
weaponization, and also the kind of intelligence color that's with guarantee that if they decide
to break out towards nuclear weapons, it's going to be undetectable. So these things, I think, are
among the factors that make the leadership kind of more cautious, and still pushing them towards
thinking and seeing the nuclear program more as a leverage than an actual means for deterrence.
And here, of course, comes the question of the state of war most. So I think what
prevented the Iranian leadership from going towards the weaponization past is more the
two factors that I mentioned, the lack of proper infrastructure and also intelligence exposure
than the leverage that they have achieved in the state of war most. Because again, the arguments
within the Indian debates regarding the state of war most is that, of course, we found this
very important, very significant leverage that we always had, actually, given our geography,
what we weren't aware of. And it's really played a significant role in helping us end the war
in our terms somehow. But still, this leverage can gradually be weakened, and even if not
rendered irrelevant, at least less significant in the future. And I think they have a point here
because, look, already, Saudi Arabia and the UAE have pipelines that bypass the state of
war most, right? Each of them being able to ship at least five million barrels of oil per day.
Saudi Arabia did this throughout the war and under sheeps fire. For UAE, it was more difficult
because Iran continued its strikes against UAE. And so there is a kind of a consensus on the
argument that the straits was the single most important leverage that Iran had in this war.
But there is much less consensus on whether the same leverage can be used if there is a war in say
like 10 years, 20 years, or something like that. So does that also imply that Iran remains
just as, if not more incentivized to maintain its extra resistance, including its backing of the
Houthis, if the solution for the Saudis and the Emirates to an Iranian closure of the
Strait of Hormuz is to divert more of their exports through the Red Sea. First of all, one thing
I think which became clear is what's clear as to me is for many people before the war as well,
but this war, and especially the way that Iran treated Hasbullah and the Lebanon issue,
what it proves was that this relationship, the relationship between Iran and many of these
groups can actually be described as pace from proxy relationship because in a pace from proxy
relationship, you don't enter into a war to support your proxies, the proxies to fight for you.
This was exactly the opposite of what happened just a couple of weeks ago when Iran decided to
fire missiles toward Israel in retaliation for Israeli strikes on Dahia in southern Beirut and
threaten to do that again on the same day that the memorandum understanding was eventually announced.
Regardless of how successful a phrase was or not, the strategic message was that at least as far
as Iran is concerned, these groups are seen as allies. And when it comes to the Houthis,
it's actually more so because the Houthis don't even have the same ideological
nationality as Allah has with Iran or some of the Iraqi Shia factions have. I mean, the Zaid
face that the Houthis have is considered to be kind of a branch of Shiaism, but it's not
exactly the same as the 12th Shiaism as Iran has. And also, look, when it comes to the
this concept of the access of resistance, the network of Iranian partners and proxies,
the Houthis are kind of late commerce. They started their coordination with the Iran
network in 2015. So in that sense, it's very much, and here's my point, when it comes to the
Red Sea dynamics, it's very much kind of affected by the dynamics between the Houthis and Saudi Arabia
as much as it is, if not more, as the Iran Saudi dynamics, for example, or the Iranian consideration.
So there were already reports that Saudi Arabia was in close contact not only with Iran,
but also with the Houthis, offering them even assistance in order to dissuade them from
closing the Red Sea and Babel Mandar so that Saudi Arabia could do its business. I still do believe
that in a scenario of the resumption of the war, like the full-scale war, and if Iran actually
asked the Houthis to disrupt the Babel Mandar and the Red Sea at risk partially, they would do that
for their own ideological and also strategic considerations. But that didn't happen because Iran
concluded that they could do, do is on their own, based on their source of own control over
the straights or most. But again, the Red Sea dynamics, of course, are also going to be
important, but with this caveat that it's going to be about more than just Iran itself and other
actors are there to play different roles, depending on the circumstances. So just a few more
questions about the MOU before we proceed to a more in-depth discussion about the changing nature
of the Iranian regime, as well as the implications for Israel, the Gulf States, Saudi Arabia, and China,
which I know you have some informed opinions about given your writings on China's role in Central Asia.
How does what is being discussed now compared to what was agreed upon originally in the joint
comprehensive plan of action that Trump, you know, laterally withdrew from in 2018 and which
served as the basis of his primary criticism about the state of U.S. Iranian relations and
what he sought to change in the course of the last two years, and especially with Operation
Epic Fury. So, first of all, regarding the kind of criticism of the Obama administration that
the JCPOA basically provided billions or hundreds of billions of cash to Iran, there is a fallacy here
that's we need to be aware of. What the deal actually did, I mean, there was actually sanctions
relief, but it was both on freezing Iran's own funds, which was frozen due to the sanctions,
both the United States or the U.S. sanctions and also the international sanctions. So the
distinction matters, why? Because now the exact same provisions are mentioned in this
Noirondo understanding. Also, Iranian frozen funds are going to be released. So, that's
important. Neither under Obama nor now under Trump, and I emphasize this so that there wouldn't
be any misunderstanding that I'm taking these sides, you know, comparing to that one.
Under neither of these administrations, but they're so far, there has been any
arrangements or any agreements of transferring U.S. money to Iran. So, that's an
important distinction here. Second, when it's also kind of the details of the document, trying
again, keeping in mind that what we have right now is the Marondo understanding. It's going to be
just the first steps towards a broader deal in the very semistic scenario. There are some
similarities. There are some differences. The main difference is that this agreement,
the current MOU is about more than just a nuclear program. JCPOA was strictly about the
alien nuclear program, the details of it, Iran's, I mean, timetables about kind of the lifting of
restrictions and also step-by-step sanctions removal, everything that had to do with the nuclear
program, nuclear related activities, nuclear related sanctions. This one, as I said, it starts
by a commitment of refusing to resort to force or threat of force or something. So, again,
in the very optimistic scenario that this deal becomes finalized, it's going to be about much
more than the nuclear program. It can be, again, I really don't think it's going to happen for
the reasons that we can't discuss, but it can potentially be stepping a stone for a transform
relationship between Iran and the United States. And then, when it comes to the actual, so that's
about the scope of it's bigger than just the nuclear program, it also has to do with the international
shipping and everything. Then, about the actual details of the nuclear program. So, look, as you
mentioned, the JCPOA was negotiated over a course of more than four years. And there was every
single detail about the number of centrifuges, the type of the centrifuges, the time that each
generation of centrifuges could or could not come to the enrichment circle. Or, I don't know,
like the actual steps that Iran should take in terms of granting access, for example,
and this or that facility to the IAA inspectors, it was a very, very detailed deal.
And the Obama administration, if I remember correctly, went to great lengths to explain those
details to the public. They thought they were important, not just for the deal itself, but also
for their ability to sell it. Yeah, absolutely. I mean, even for me at the time, when I looked at
the text of the JCPOA, which was kind of quite extensive. I have experts on international security
and Iran's foreign policy. I only reviewed those parts about the outline of the steps on the
Iranian side and the sanctions removal, etc., because I simply don't know about the details of
the centrifugial answer. It was very technical. Now, none of the details related to the commitment
on the Iranian side, regarding the nuclear program, nor the actual mechanisms for the sanctions
removal, for example, this kind of step by step mechanism. None of these, you can see in the
current NOU. It's a kind of promise by both sides that Iran should take steps to clarify the,
if the stockpile of highly enriched uranium, and also the future of its nuclear program,
and so on and so forth, and then the United States will also remove sanctions and allow Iran
access to its funds and things like that. So in a way, I was framist as the compares to the
JCPOA, the outline of the deal that is presented in the system OU is going to be something like
more for more. When you say more for more, you mean the Iranians give more and return for more?
Exactly. What I'm going to say doesn't exist in the text itself, but this is something that
I've seen and I've heard from various sources, you know, speaking about this. So Iran is going to
commit to stopping enrichment for a certain period of time. That period of time is still
undecided. So on the U.S. side, there was a demand for 20 years or at least 16 years,
the Iranians were saying 10 years, but in any case, this is something that didn't exist on the
JCPOA. Under the JCPOA since day one, Iran was allowed to enrich at 3.6 percent enrichment level.
So Iran is going to stop enrichment for a certain period of time, and also delutes. It's not
quite a highly enriched uranium, probably increasing inspections, more sort of an intensive
measures to make sure that it's not going to break out towards urbanization, and then there's
going to be no path to urbanization. And on the U.S. side, there are notions in the text itself
that should be deal, be finalized. The United States is going to remove not only the secondary
sanctions, but even the primary sanctions, and also the kind of investment fund that is green
lights it by the United States to help Iran rebuild its economy. President Trump says there's
going to be no U.S. money in it. We don't know. But in any case, there's more economic
incentives, financial incentives for Iran, and any strange Iran is supposed to take more
serious or better to say kind of to make more commitments regarding this nuclear program.
It's still far from President Trump's long-standing demand that Iran's nuclear program be dismantled,
but it's still, when it comes to nuclear, it's going to be, if finalized, it's going to be
much stricter than the JCPOI, I believe. So Iran has insisted on linking Lebanon to any
deal with strikes with the United States, which is obviously a major security concern for Israel.
How does this dimension of the conflict and the persistent threat that has been
opposed to Israel through its operational presence in Lebanon, as we've already discussed,
in the course of this conversation, threaten the viability of whatever is ultimately agreed to?
Which also brings in the larger question of what role does Israel really have in the course
of these negotiations, and in terms of being in a position to undermine any peace agreement
that the U.S. and Iran ultimately come to? That's, I think, the most complicated part of the
MOU, and also the part that has the biggest potential for kind of disrupting this whole thing.
Not Israel itself, but the insistence to link these two sort of arenas together, Lebanon
and Iran. And actually, it was the day before yesterday, I'm not mistaken, but the Iranian
the spokesperson for Iran's foreign ministry went even further, saying that this war had four
parties on the one side. It was us, like Iran, and Hasbullah, and on the other side, the U.S. and
Israel. So every any arrangement, any kind of an agreement is going to be binding for Israel as well,
and that the Islamic Republic won't accept excuses that Israel is going to, you know, act independently
and so on and so forth. So on the one hand, the very fact that Iran managed to include Lebanon,
when this case is partner Hasbullah, in a deal with the United States, and also on the ground,
source of it's trying to validate its red line of Israeli attack on Hasbullah,
being mad swiss, Iranian response. That can be seen in other success, right? And a kind of
affirmation of Iran's renewed source of original status in the sense that it can be clear for
listeners, you're referring to a recent retaliation by Iran on Israel in response to Israeli attacks
in Lebanon, going after Hasbullah. And as I understand it, that was the first time that the
Iranians had ever struck Israel in response to Israel striking a third-party territory and not
Iran itself. Yeah, yeah, absolutely. Which you think was also meant to signal their commitment
to the Lebanese issue. To signal their commitments, and also to
some of the signal that Iran is now a more assertive, more influential,
